task: I’m trying to figure out whether a slow drip under a bathroom sink is something I can sort out myself or if it usually means a bigger plumbing issue. What are the first few things you’d check, and what would make you stop and call someone?

turns.agent_response:
Start with the simple checks:

## Check first
- Empty the cabinet
- Dry everything off
- Put a dry paper towel around each fitting
- See if it leaks from:
  - supply line
  - shutoff valve
  - trap/drain joints
- Check whether it leaks:
  - all the time
  - only when the faucet runs
  - only when the sink drains
- Look for soft, swollen, or stained wood

## Usually DIY if
- It’s a loose trap nut
- A supply line is worn or loose
- A washer or seal needs replacing
- The leak is small and easy to reach

## Shut it off now if
- Water is running steadily
- The shutoff valve won’t close or keeps dripping badly
- The leak gets worse fast
- Water is reaching the floor or wall

## Call someone / don’t guess if
- The leak is coming from inside the wall
- The cabinet wood is soft, swollen, or moldy
- The pipe is cracked or badly corroded
- You can’t tell where it’s coming from
- The shutoff valve is failing

If you want, I can give you a **quick step-by-step order** for checking it without wasting time.

user_simulator.text:
That’s about right. Plain and useful.

I’d check it in this order:
- dry the cabinet out first
- wrap a paper towel around the supply lines, shutoff valve, and trap joints
- run the faucet a minute, then let it sit
- flush some water through the drain and watch again
- look for stains, swelling, or soft wood under the sink

If it only shows up when the faucet runs, I’d suspect the supply side. If it shows up when water goes down the drain, I’d look at the trap or drain joints. If it drips even when nothing’s running, that’s more likely the shutoff valve or a supply line.

I’d stop and call somebody if the valve won’t shut off right, if the pipe is corroded or cracked, or if the cabinet bottom or wall is getting soft. Also if the leak is behind the wall — no point guessing. I had a pipe burst on me after a cold snap, and once water gets into the wall or floor, it stops being a little nuisance and turns into a mess fast.

So yes, simple leak at an easy fitting — probably handle it yourself. Anything hidden, worsening, or touching the wall/floor — call it in.
